comments:
Summary:
This paper introduces and analyzes a new affine measurement model for low-rank matrix recovery in which each measurement is a linear combination of entries from a single row or a single column (“row-and-column” measurements). Two ensembles are studied: RCMC (sampling entire rows/columns, i.e. single-entry measurements concentrated on rows/cols) and GRC (Gaussian random combinations of rows/columns). The authors propose a simple practical algorithm SVLS (SVD followed by least-squares, with an optional local gradient refinement), and provide theory: exact recovery with the information-theoretic minimum number of measurements for GRC in the noiseless case, high-probability recovery for RCMC under incoherence (with logarithmic factor), and Frobenius-norm error bounds in the noisy GRC case. Extensive simulations compare SVLS+designs against standard measurement ensembles and recovery algorithms (nuclear-norm minimization, OptSpace, SVT), showing faster runtime and comparable or better accuracy in many regimes. Code is made available.

Strengths:
- The measurement model is natural for many applications (e.g., experimental setups that measure interactions of a single probe across all targets) and differs substantially from standard GE/MC models; studying it is worthwhile.
- The SVLS algorithm is simple, computationally efficient, and practically appealing. Its use of grouped measurements (rows/columns) yields storage and computation advantages.
- The theoretical contributions are substantial: exact recovery result for GRC at the information-theoretic minimum measurements, RCMC guarantees under incoherence, and a concrete noisy-case error bound for GRC comparable in form to GE results.
- Empirical evaluation is extensive and supports the claims; code availability is a plus.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Some theoretical statements hide numerical constants (e.g., c, cR, cC) and conditions (k ≥ max(4r,40) etc.). Explicit dependence on problem parameters and constants (or at least sharper quantification) would help assess practical settings.
- The noisy-case bounds rely on spectral-norm bounds of noise and on random Gaussian structure; it would be helpful to discuss dependence on condition numbers of X (singular value decay) and on whether the algorithm degrades for poorly conditioned low-rank matrices.
- Rank estimation is handled via the elbow heuristic. While experiments indicate it works well, more discussion or experiments about robustness to rank mis-specification (especially when singular values decay gradually) would be valuable.
- The RCMC analysis requires incoherence (as in standard MC). It would help to clarify classes of matrices for which RCMC is preferable to MC in practice, and to present empirical tests on structured matrices (e.g., spiky or coherent cases) to better illustrate regimes of advantage.
- Comparisons are made to SVT/OptSpace and to GE-based nuclear-norm minimization. A direct empirical comparison to recently proposed related measurement models (e.g., rank-one projections or other structured sketches) would strengthen the experimental section (the paper cites such related work but experiments are limited).
- The gradient-descent refinement is only briefly described; more guidance on its convergence or typical improvement would be useful.
- The provided manuscript text had some repetitions/typos in places (likely typesetting extraction artifacts). A cleanup would improve readability.

Minor comments / suggestions:
- Add an explicit complexity analysis (time and memory) for SVLS as a function of n1,n2,k,r to complement the empirical speed claims.
- Discuss behavior when only rows or only columns are available (the paper mentions rows alone is insufficient) and possible hybrid sampling strategies.
- If possible, include empirical sensitivity to non-Gaussian noise or outliers.

Overall evaluation and recommendation:
This paper studies an original and practically motivated measurement model, provides a simple and effective algorithm, proves meaningful theoretical guarantees (including an optimal-measurements exact recovery result for the Gaussian rows/columns model), and validates the approach empirically. The work is solid and useful to the low-rank recovery community. Some aspects could be expanded (constants, rank-mis-specification, more structured empirical comparisons), but they do not detract from the main contributions. I recommend acceptance / dissemination (strong accept).
